slides.title: Beyond the Blueprint
slides.subtitle: The Web was built to share physics papers. What happened next is the exam's favorite story about intent, misuse, and scale
slides.footerLeft: AP Computer Science Principles · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2023)
slides.script: Day 2 of Beneficial and Harmful Effects. Yesterday was about effects and who judges them; today is about intent — what happens when an innovation is used for things its creators never imagined. The CED tells this story through three named examples every AP student must know: the World Wide Web, targeted advertising, and machine learning with data mining. Then it draws a careful line: responsible programmers must try to foresee unintended uses, and no programmer can foresee them all. Today also launches Innovation Investigation #3 — by the end of class each student has an innovation and an outline.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain how a computing innovation can have an impact beyond its intended purpose (LO IOC-1.B)
Explain what responsible programmers can — and cannot — do about unintended uses at scale (LO IOC-1.B)

slides.script: Two targets, both inside LO IOC-1.B. First, impact beyond intent: using the CED's three named examples to show how innovations outgrow their blueprints, for better and for worse. Second, the responsibility line: what the College Board expects of responsible programmers, why complete foresight is impossible, and how rapid sharing at scale moves impacts beyond anyone's control. Everything today feeds directly into Innovation Investigation #3.

slides.prompt: In 1989, Tim Berners-Lee built the World Wide Web so physicists could share research papers quickly and easily.
slides.subprompt: List 4 things the Web does today that its inventor never planned. Circle the one that would surprise him most, and note who it helps — and who it hurts.
slides.directions: Be ready to share your thinking.
slides.script: Pick up yesterday's teaser. The Web had one intended purpose: rapid, easy exchange of information within the scientific community. Four minutes: list four things the Web does today that were nowhere in that plan, circle the biggest surprise, and note who it helps and who it hurts. Shopping, streaming, social media, banking, scams, doxxing — every answer is evidence for today's Essential Knowledge: computing innovations get used in ways their creators had not originally intended. Keep the list; the first worked table formalizes it.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 2 Guided Notes have 3 sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required.

sections.title: Uses Beyond Intent
sections.description: The Web, targeted advertising, and machine learning: intended purpose vs. what actually happened (EK IOC-1.B.1, B.6).

sections.title: Responsibility and Its Limits
sections.description: Harm to society, economy, and culture; what responsible programmers do; what nobody can foresee (EK IOC-1.B.2–B.5).

sections.title: Deep Dive: GPS, Missiles to Meals
sections.description: How a Cold War weapons system became farming, dating, and dinner-delivery infrastructure.

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. Absent or lost your packet? A digital copy lives on the Topic 5.1 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com.
slides.script: Today's Guided Notes map: two green CB-required sections — uses beyond intent, with the CED's three named examples, and the responsibility line — plus a blue GPS deep-dive for the enrichment track. Keep the packet open and fill as we go; a digital copy lives on the Topic 5.1 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com for anyone absent or missing a packet.

slides.label: Uses Beyond Intent
slides.kicker: The CED's Three Named Examples · Learning Objective IOC-1.B
slides.script: Section one. The College Board does something unusual in this topic: instead of leaving examples to the teacher, it names three specific innovations inside the Essential Knowledge itself — the World Wide Web, targeted advertising, and machine learning with data mining. Exam items quote these stories almost verbatim, so we learn them as the CED tells them: intended purpose first, then what actually happened.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO IOC-1.B)

terms.definition: The job an innovation's creators designed it to do; computing innovations can be used in ways their creators had not originally intended (EK IOC-1.B.1).
Ads matched to individuals using collected data — used to help businesses, but it can be misused at both individual and aggregate levels (EK IOC-1.B.1).
Programs that improve at a task by finding patterns in data; with data mining it has enabled innovation in medicine, business, and science (EK IOC-1.B.1).
Searching large data sets for hidden patterns; information discovered this way has also been used to discriminate against groups of individuals (EK IOC-1.B.1).
Spreading a program quickly or running it with a large number of users — impacts can then exceed the intended purpose or control of the programmer (EK IOC-1.B.6).

slides.script: Five terms, all pulled from the wording of EK IOC-1.B.1 and B.6. Intended purpose is the baseline every beyond-intent story is measured against. Targeted advertising carries a two-sided definition on purpose — helps businesses, misused at individual AND aggregate levels — and the exam tests both sides. Machine learning and data mining travel as a pair in the CED: pattern-finding that enabled innovation in medicine, business, and science, and also produced discrimination against groups. Rapid sharing is the scale term — we return to it in a few slides. Every definition goes into the packet.

slides.heading: Three Innovations, Two Stories
slides.context: The CED names three innovations whose real story outgrew the plan. For each: what was intended, and what happened beyond it?
rows: Rapid, easy exchange of information within the scientific community
Commerce, streaming, social media — the platform of modern life
Help businesses reach the customers most likely to buy
Misused at individual AND aggregate levels — profiling and manipulation
Enable innovation in medicine, business, and science
Discovered information used to discriminate against groups of individuals
slides.script: The three named stories, in CED language. Row one: the Web's intended purpose was information exchange within the scientific community — everything else on your bell-ringer list is the beyond-intent column. Row two: targeted advertising exists to help businesses, and the CED flags misuse at two distinct levels — individual, one person profiled and pressured, and aggregate, whole groups steered at once; keep those levels separate, the exam does. Row three carries the sharpest edge in the unit: the same pattern-finding that advances medicine has been used to discriminate against groups of individuals. Students fill the blanked cells as we go — these exact phrases are worth memorizing.

slides.heading: Scale Changes the Story
bullets: Some ways innovations get used have a harmful impact on SOCIETY, the ECONOMY, or CULTURE — damage bigger than any one victim (EK IOC-1.B.2).
Rapid sharing of a program — or running it with a large number of users — creates impacts beyond the intended purpose or the programmer's control (EK IOC-1.B.6).
A flaw touching 0.1% of users is 100 people at launch and 100,000 after a viral month — scale turns edge cases into headlines.
Surprises point both ways: innovations often have unintended BENEFICIAL effects by leading to advances in other fields (EK IOC-1.B.5).
slides.script: Two Essential Knowledge statements about size, one about direction. First, harm is not only personal: the CED names society, the economy, and culture as arenas of harmful impact — lost job categories, discriminated groups, reshaped habits — no single named victim required. Second, rapid sharing: when a program spreads faster than its makers can react, or runs with an enormous user base, the impacts move beyond the intended purpose and beyond the programmer's control — the word 'control' is the one to underline. The arithmetic bullet makes it concrete: the same tiny flaw rate becomes a headline at scale. And B.5 is the bright side of surprise — unintended effects include gifts to other fields, not just damage.

capture.q: Harmful impact isn't only personal — it can land on ______.
capture.a: whole communities, markets, and shared ways of life

capture.q: Programmers lose control of impacts when ______.
capture.a: distribution outruns the team — adoption moves faster than any response

capture.q: Scale converts a tiny flaw into a crisis by ______.
capture.a: multiplying rare events until they happen constantly somewhere

slides.label: Responsibility and Its Limits
slides.kicker: Foreseeable vs. Impossible · Learning Objective IOC-1.B
slides.script: Section two. If innovations always escape their blueprints, what exactly do the people who build them owe the rest of us? The College Board's answer is a carefully drawn line — two Essential Knowledge statements that look like they contradict each other and don't. Responsible programmers try to consider unintended uses; no programmer can consider them all. The exam tests whether students can hold both at once.

slides.heading: Responsible, Not All-Knowing
slides.subheading: The CED draws a careful line: trying to foresee misuse is required; succeeding completely is impossible (EK IOC-1.B.3–B.4).

left.title: What responsible programmers DO
left.definition: Try to consider unintended uses and their beneficial and harmful effects
examples: Brainstorm misuse before launch: 'how could this hurt someone?'
Build guardrails — reporting tools, rate limits, privacy defaults
Watch how the product is really used, and respond after release

right.title: What NO programmer can do
right.definition: Consider all the ways a computing innovation can be used
examples: Users outnumber the team a million to one — so do their ideas
Tomorrow's contexts — devices, laws, cultures — don't exist yet
Some impacts only appear at a scale no test can simulate

slides.footer: AP TIP: An option claiming a programmer “considered every possible use” — or should have — is wrong. The standard is effort, not omniscience.
slides.script: Two columns, one line between duty and impossibility. The left column is EK IOC-1.B.3's standard, and the verb matters: responsible programmers TRY — brainstorming misuse before launch, shipping guardrails, and staying engaged after release when reality starts voting. The right column is EK IOC-1.B.4's limit, stated absolutely: it is NOT POSSIBLE to consider all the ways an innovation can be used, and the three examples explain why — the crowd out-thinks the team, the future supplies contexts that don't exist yet, and some effects only emerge at scale. On the exam, any option claiming complete foresight happened or should have happened is wrong on sight.

capture.q: The CED's responsibility standard is ______, not perfection.
capture.a: a genuine attempt to imagine misuse ahead of time and prepare for it
the crowd's inventiveness always outgrows what a small team can list
observing actual behavior and correcting course when new harms surface

slides.heading: “Responsible Programmers Catch Every Misuse”
slides.misconception: If a team is truly responsible, it will find every possible misuse before launch — any harm that slips through proves the programmers were careless.
slides.whyItHappens: In hindsight every misuse looks obvious, so post-disaster headlines read 'they should have known.'
slides.correction: Responsibility means TRYING: responsible programmers consider unintended uses and their beneficial and harmful effects (EK IOC-1.B.3). It is not possible to consider ALL the ways an innovation can be used (EK IOC-1.B.4) — millions of users invent situations no team could list.
slides.example: The Web's own creator expected physics papers, not phishing sites — no launch checklist in 1989 could have carried a line item for 'romance scams.'
slides.script: Here's the trap, and it cuts both directions. One student concludes that any harm proves negligence — that fails EK IOC-1.B.4, since complete foresight is impossible, not merely difficult. Another student concludes that since you can't catch everything, trying is pointless — that fails EK IOC-1.B.3, which makes the effort itself the standard. The stable position is the CED's: duty to try, impossibility of finishing. The Web example inoculates against hindsight bias — judge 1989 decisions by 1989 knowledge. Students explain in the packet why the myth is wrong; strong answers cite both EKs by their logic, not just one.

slides.heading: Deep Dive: GPS — Missiles to Meal Delivery
bullets: GPS was built by the U.S. military in the 1970s with one intended purpose: positioning forces and weapons anywhere on Earth.
Opened fully to civilians in 2000, it now quietly powers farm tractors, earthquake sensors, dating apps, and delivery windows.
Almost none of today's uses appeared in any defense requirements document — impact beyond intended purpose, at planetary scale (LO IOC-1.B).
The pattern generalizes: infrastructure invented for one field becomes the raw material other fields build on (EK IOC-1.B.5).
slides.script: A deep dive for the enrichment track. GPS is the cleanest beyond-intent story ever told: a Cold War weapons-positioning system that now tells a tractor where the row ends and a delivery app when dinner arrives. Two details reward attention. First, the pivot was a choice — the government switched off civilian signal degradation in 2000, and entire industries appeared within a decade. Second, the direction of surprise was positive: this is EK IOC-1.B.5's unintended beneficial effects at maximum size. When Investigation #3 topics get picked in a few minutes, GPS-dependent innovations are fair game and rich ones.

capture.q: GPS shows that an innovation's biggest uses can come from ______.
capture.a: industries that didn't exist when it was designed — outsiders repurposed it

capture.q: One-field infrastructure becomes many-field infrastructure when ______.
capture.a: others treat the tool as a foundation to construct on, not a finished product

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Launch Investigation #3
slides.subheading: Innovation Investigation #3 starts here: your innovation's purpose, its data, and its effects. Maps to LO IOC-1.A and IOC-1.B.
prompts: Choose a computing innovation to investigate and state its intended purpose in one sentence — what did its creators build it to do?
Identify the data it consumes or produces, and one way that data is — or could be — used beyond the original intent.
Describe one beneficial and one harmful effect. Is either a single effect that different people judge differently?
Name one unintended use its programmers could reasonably have considered — and one nobody could have foreseen.
slides.directions: Draft complete-sentence answers — they become the outline of your Investigation #3 write-up. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 5.1 page.
slides.script: This Stop and Think doubles as the launch of Innovation Investigation #3, the third of the course's three required investigations into computing innovations. Eight minutes. Prompt one locks the choice and the intended purpose; prompt two adds the data dimension, which the Create task and Topic 5.5 both build on; prompt three applies yesterday's dual-view test; prompt four applies today's responsibility line. These four answers ARE the outline of the write-up — the full Create-adjacent version gets dedicated work time in the flex block. Innovations from the deep-dive slide and the exercises are fair game, but each student needs their own.

slides.heading: Common AP Traps
slides.subheading: Three ways Topic 5.1 loses points on the exam — one minute now, real points in May.

strategies.name: One effect, two verdicts
strategies.text: The exam wants ONE effect judged beneficial by one viewer and harmful by another — not a good effect plus a separate bad effect. Find the single shared effect first.

strategies.name: “Considered every use” = wrong
strategies.text: EK IOC-1.B.4 is absolute: it is NOT POSSIBLE to consider all the ways an innovation can be used. Any option claiming a programmer did, could, or should have is a trap.

strategies.name: Harm isn't only personal
strategies.text: Harmful impacts can land on society, the economy, or culture (EK IOC-1.B.2) — lost jobs, group discrimination, cultural shifts. No named victim is required.

slides.script: One minute, three traps, real points. Trap one: when a question describes praise and complaints about an innovation, hunt for the single effect underneath both — options that split it into two separate effects are bait. Trap two: anticipation is impossible, not optional — B.3 requires the attempt, B.4 forbids believing the attempt can be complete; 'considered every possible use' is wrong in every costume. Trap three: don't reject a harm option just because no individual is named — society, economy, and culture are the CED's own list. Dodge these three and this topic pays for itself in May.

slides.heading: Beneficial & Harmful, in One Slide
points: People create innovations; tasks reorganize around them; creativity spreads to other fields (EK IOC-1.A.1–A.2, A.5)
Not every effect is anticipated, and one effect can be beneficial AND harmful — different people, or the same person (EK IOC-1.A.3–A.4)
The Web, targeted advertising, and machine learning all outgrew their intended purposes (EK IOC-1.B.1)
Harm can hit society, the economy, or culture — and rapid sharing at scale moves impacts beyond the programmer's control (EK IOC-1.B.2, B.6)
Responsible programmers try to consider unintended uses; considering them ALL is impossible (EK IOC-1.B.3–B.4)
slides.next: Topic 5.2, The Digital Divide: computing changed everything — but who actually gets access, and what happens to the people who don't?
ican: explain how one effect of an innovation can be both beneficial and harmful (LO IOC-1.A)
explain how innovations change tasks and spark other fields (LO IOC-1.A)
explain how an innovation's impact can go beyond its intended purpose (LO IOC-1.B)
explain what responsible programmers can — and cannot — foresee (LO IOC-1.B)
slides.script: The whole topic in one slide. Innovations are human-made, and life reorders around them; effects surprise their makers, and one effect can carry two verdicts at once. The three named stories — the Web, targeted advertising, machine learning with data mining — show impact beyond intent in both directions, and scale can move that impact beyond anyone's control. The responsibility line: try to foresee, impossible to finish. Next class opens Topic 5.2, the Digital Divide — who actually gets access to all of this. Before packing up, run the exit check: four 'I can' statements at the bottom of the slide; any unchecked box is tonight's review target on the Topic 5.1 page. Guided Notes are collected, and Investigation #3 outlines come to the flex block.
